title: Superconfined Antiferromagnons on the Two-Dimensional Penrose Lattice
abstract: We find novel confined states in the spin-$S$ nearest-neighbor antiferromagnetic Heisenberg model on the two-dimensional Penrose lattice. Linear spin waves have massively degenerate eigenstates strictly confined to tricoordinated sites. They contrast with the well-known itinerant analogs in the tight-binding model, where electrons are confined but extended to both tricoordinated and pentacoordinated sites. It is the site potentials in the spin-wave Hamiltonian, originating from Coulomb interactions between electrons, that confine spin waves to minimally coordinated sites only. Confined states in the tight-binding Hamiltonian consist of six types of building blocks, whereas those in the spin-wave Hamiltonian consist of only four of them. Confined spin waves are robust against $1/S$ corrections. Emergent $O(S^{0})$ interactions further confine -- superconfine -- spin waves into two separate groups within tricoordinated sites.
summary: This paper identifies a phenomenon called 'superconfinement' in a quasiperiodic Penrose lattice, where antiferromagnetic excitations are more strictly localized than electrons. It demonstrates how quantum interactions between magnons lift the macroscopic degeneracy of these confined states. The findings highlight how aperiodic geometry and site potentials uniquely govern collective excitations.
main_problem: The study investigates the nature of novel confined states (antiferromagnons) in a two-dimensional Penrose lattice and compares their spatial confinement to electrons in a tight-binding model.
main_result: The authors discover 'superconfinement,' where spin-wave excitations are restricted strictly to tricoordinated sites, and show that quantum corrections (magnon-magnon interactions) lift the massive degeneracy of these states into two separate groups.
method: The study employs Linear Spin-Wave (LSW) theory and an Interacting Spin-Wave (ISW) formalism using the Holstein-Primakoff transformation, combined with perpendicular-space analysis and numerical diagonalization of periodic approximants.
model_or_system: A spin-S nearest-neighbor antiferromagnetic Heisenberg model on a two-dimensional bipartite Penrose lattice, analyzed using both LSW and ISW Hamiltonians.
key_observables: Site-resolved density of states (DOS), wavefunction amplitudes, spectral weight distribution, and coordination-number-resolved DOS.
important_parameters: Spin magnitude (S), exchange interaction (J), coordination number (z), and the golden ratio (tau).
main_assumptions: The analysis is primarily based on the Holstein-Primakoff boson expansion truncated at the O(S^0) level.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the Penrose lattice structure; Figure 2 and 3 display coordination-resolved DOS and site-resolved amplitudes; Figure 4 illustrates building blocks; Figure 7 compares LSW and ISW spectra; Figure 9-11 detail the lattice projection and vertex types.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the Penrose lattice and the Heisenberg model, compares LSW and tight-binding confinement, analyzes the construction of confined states via building blocks, investigates the impact of quantum corrections (ISW), and concludes with the effects of boundary conditions and local environments.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) A two-dimensional Penrose lattice with size L = 526 and (b) an enlarged view of a speciﬁc region. In the enlarged view (b), the open and ﬁlled circles represent sites belonging to the two sublattices. (c) Two types of rhombic prototiles constituting the Penrose lattice, with acute angles of π

Figure 2 shows the coordination-number-resolved density of states ρz(ω), where z ranges from 3 to 7 (see Ap- pendix A). The spectral weight shifts toward sites with higher coordination numbers as the energy increases, a feature attributable to the immobility of spin deviations in the Ising limit. In this limit, the excitation spectrum con- sists of discrete eigenlevels at zJS. The correlation between coordination number and excitation energy persists even when the degeneracy is lifted by spin ﬂuctuations. Notably, the sharp peak at ℏω = 3JS indicates macroscopically degenerate conﬁned states.

FIG. 2. The coordination-number-resolved density of states ρz(ω) for the LSW excitation spectrum on the Penrose lattice with L = 11006, where z ranges from 3 to 7. Note that z = 2 vertices are artifacts of ﬁnite open-boundary clusters.

Figure 3 demonstrates the spatial conﬁnement of degenerate states at ε∓ k = 3JS for the LSW Hamiltonian and at εk = 0 for the tight-binding Hamiltonian (see Appendix B) on the Penrose lattice. Here, εk denotes the eigenvalues of the tight-binding Hamiltonian. In both cases, these conﬁned states are spatially separated by “forbidden ladders” [29, 34], which are one-dimensional, unbranched closed loops with zero wavefunction amplitude. Within each domain enclosed by these forbidden ladders, the conﬁned states exhibit nonzero amplitudes exclusively on either the A or B sublattice. The alternating pattern of conﬁnement domains across the forbidden ladders matches perfectly between Figures 3a...

FIG. 3. The site-resolved densities of states ρl(ω) [Equations (14) and (B4)] multiplied by the number of sites L = 11006 as functions of site position rl. The lth site is marked with a red (l ∈A) or blue (l ∈B) dot when Lρl(ω) is larger than or equal to 10−2. At every unmarked site l, Lρl(ω) < 10−15. (a) Lρl(ω) at ℏω = 3JS for the LSW Hamiltonian (13). (b) Lρl(ω) at ℏω = 0 for the tight-binding Hamiltonian (B3). Rectangles highlight regions enlarged in the bottom panels and in Figure 13.
